original_abstract: Skeletal editing has emerged as a transformative paradigm in synthetic chemistry, offering a surgical approach to the direct remodeling of molecular cores. By enabling the insertion, deletion, or exchange of specific atoms within a molecular framework, this strategy bypasses the resource-intensive requirements of traditional de novo synthesis and streamlines the late-stage diversification of lead compounds. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of recent breakthroughs in the skeletal editing of (hetero)cyclic systems, with a primary focus on single-atom modifications. We categorize the field into three fundamental strategies: ring expansion via atom insertion, ring contraction through atom removal, and atom-swapping transmutations. By contextualizing modern methods—such as carbene-mediated carbon insertion and photo-induced nitrogen deletion—within their historical mechanistic roots, we highlight how these tools are being harnessed to explore chemical space with unprecedented precision. Furthermore, we discuss the burgeoning areas of isotopic exchange and stereochemical editing, addressing current challenges in selectivity and sustainability while outlining the future potential of "molecular surgery" in drug discovery and materials science.
humanized_abstract: Skeletal editing: a surgical approach to structure editing through atom replacement or insertion skeletal editing recently emerged as a new methodology in synthetic chemistry for the design of molecules with minimal changes through the insertion, deletion or replacement of a single atom into, from or within a preformed scaffold that enables the syntheses to be highly economic and for the late stage modification of a lead molecule. Within this review, we provide an overview of the recent advancements within the skeletal editing field with respect to (hetero)cyclic structures and focuses primarily on the single atom editing of a molecule core. A taxonomy of approaches within the skeletal editing has been divided into three categories including ring expansion through atom insertion, ring contraction via atom removal and atom swap transmutations. A perspective that links present day transformations with historical mechanisms that permitted the development of these transformative tools, exemplifying their applicability within the skeleton editing method to explore, in unprecedented detail, a large segment of chemical space and, additionally, elucidates concepts within the relatively new fields of isotopic editing and stereochemical editing; discussing, in addition, challenges that need to be addressed in order for the field of molecular surgery to translate into clinical significance as well as materials advancement.
